food_name: Eggplant with Garlic Sauce (Yu Xiang Qie Zi)
aliases: Sichuan Eggplant with Spicy Garlic Sauce

english: Sichuan Eggplant with Spicy Garlic Sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general eggplant food product class (FOODON_03311737); no specific entry for Yu Xiang Qie Zi (Szechuan braised eggplant with garlic sauce). Closest parent match used as the most defensible available anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers cooked aubergine/eggplant as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for the Szechuan preparation with garlic-chili sauce. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Yu Xiang Qie Zi or Szechuan eggplant dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or home serving of Eggplant with Garlic Sauce is approximately 250 g, typically served alongside steamed rice as part of a shared Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eggplant, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (NDB 11210) and composite estimation for Szechuan-style preparation with oil and sauce
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Yu Xiang Qie Zi exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database. Values are adapted from USDA cooked eggplant (NDB 11210) as the base, with upward adjustments for fat (deep-frying oil absorption), sodium (doubanjiang and soy sauce), sugar (added sugar in sauce), and protein (minor contribution from sauce ingredients). The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this adaptation and have been retained with cross-reference to USDA data. Vitamin B12 at 0.1 mcg per 100 g is noted as atypical for a plant-based dish; this likely reflects trace contamination from fermented ingredients (doubanjiang) and is retained at low confidence.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Eggplant and Mixed Vegetable Dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked eggplant and mixed stir-fried vegetable dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Yu Xiang Qie Zi (Szechuan eggplant with garlic sauce) is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. Eggplant has a very low GI (approximately 15 for raw/boiled). The composite dish GI is estimated at 40 (low) to account for added sugar, cornstarch thickener, and oil in the sauce. Oil and dietary fibre from eggplant slow carbohydrate absorption. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 23.75) / 100 = 9.5 (low GL). This is a defensible analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg per 100 g; approximately 875 mg per 250 g serving) from doubanjiang (fermented chili bean paste) and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-sauce or steamed versions to lower sodium intake; a single 250 g serving provides approximately 38% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated 40) and moderate carbohydrate content (9.5 g per 100 g), partially offset by added sugar (4.5 g per 100 g) in the sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low GI and high dietary fibre (2.2 g per 100 g) support moderate blood glucose response; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the 4.5 g added sugar per 100 g in the sauce and pair this dish with a lean protein source rather than additional refined carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (10.8 g per 100 g) from deep-frying oil absorption, high sodium (350 mg per 100 g), and negligible cholesterol (plant-based dish)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is cholesterol-free and eggplant provides anthocyanin antioxidants beneficial for cardiovascular health; however, the high oil content from deep-frying and elevated sodium from fermented condiments warrant portion control for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and steamed or air-fried preparation is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (garlic, ginger, scallions) and eggplant anthocyanins, offset by nightshade sensitivity risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Garlic, ginger, and scallions provide anti-inflammatory compounds; however, individuals with known nightshade (Solanaceae) sensitivity should exercise caution as eggplant belongs to this plant family.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Eggplant with Garlic Sauce, known in Chinese as 鱼香茄子 (Yúxiāng qiézi, literally 'Fish-Fragrant Eggplant'), is a classic Szechuan Chinese dish originating from Sichuan Province. Despite its name, the dish contains no fish; 'fish-fragrant' (yuxiang) refers to a traditional Szechuan seasoning profile historically used in fish cookery, combining salty, sweet, sour, and spicy flavour dimensions. The dish is prepared by deep-frying long Chinese eggplants to achieve a creamy, oil-saturated interior and preserved purple exterior, followed by braising or stir-frying in a complex sauce made from doubanjiang (fermented chili bean paste), soy sauce, sugar, black vinegar, cornstarch, and generous quantities of minced garlic, ginger, and scallions. The result is meltingly tender eggplant coated in a glossy, deeply aromatic, savoury-sweet-spicy glaze. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 145 kcal, 2.5 g protein, 10.8 g fat (predominantly from frying oil), and 9.5 g carbohydrate with 2.2 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is high at 350 mg per 100 g due to fermented condiments, making it a concern for hypertension management. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low), supported by eggplant fibre and oil slowing carbohydrate absorption, making it moderately suitable for Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled. The dish is naturally cholesterol-free and vegan when prepared without pork, and eggplant provides anthocyanin antioxidants with potential cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. It is a staple order at Szechuan restaurants and commonly served alongside steamed rice at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Yu Xiang Qie Zi exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, or other approved primary sources. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA cooked eggplant (NDB 11210) with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption, sauce sodium, and added sugar, cross-referenced against the AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database principles. Medium confidence reflects the absence of a direct approved-source match for this composite Szechuan dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant (NDB 11210) as the closest approved-source equivalent; no exact entry for Yu Xiang Qie Zi exists in any approved primary source. Input AI-generated values retained where consistent with USDA adaptation. 2. Vitamin B12 at 0.1 mcg per 100 g is atypical for a plant-based dish; this likely reflects trace amounts from fermented ingredients (doubanjiang) and is retained at low confidence pending laboratory verification. 3. saturated_fat_g (1.2 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (6.5 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.5 g) are estimated from typical vegetable oil fatty acid profiles used in deep-frying; these values are not directly reported in the input and are approximations. 4. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite deep-fried and braised dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central for the specific preparation. 5. trans_fat_g set to 0.0 and cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: this is a plant-based dish with no animal fat; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for vegetable-oil-fried eggplant. 6. The duplicate portion entry in the input (two identical '1 serving (250g)' entries) has been deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. 7. Dietary tags 'low_calorie' and 'low_sugar' from the input are not fully supported given 145 kcal/100 g and 4.5 g sugar/100 g; these tags have been omitted from the output classification. 8. The input source 'Sichuan Academy of Agricultural Sciences' (http://www.saas.sh.cn) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence for nutrient values. 9. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (40 × 9.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 10000 = 9.5 (low GL). 10. Ontology match_type values corrected from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' for both foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type to conform to the permitted enumeration ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] as required by the validator.
